controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 7060030.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday September 2, 2020 at 00:02

did.unittitle: Wagner-Jenkins Correspondence
did.abstract: Correspondence between the families of Richard Wagner and Newell Sill Jenkins.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Newell Jenkins (grandson of Newell Sill Jenkins) in 1986.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open to researchers by appointment. There are no restricted materials in the collection. Please contact the Special Collections staff to schedule an appointment.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Wagner-Jenkins Correspondence is the physical property of the Irving S. Gilmore Music Library of Yale University. Copyrights belong to the composers and authors, or their legal heirs and assigns.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Misc. Ms. 345, The Wagner-Jenkins Correspondence in the Irving S. Gilmore Music Library of Yale University.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Dr. Newell Sill Jenkins (1840-1919) was an American dentist who spent most of his career in Europe, practicing chiefly in Dresden. He had many notable patients, including the composer Richard Wagner and his family. The Wagner family and the Jenkins family became friends, and they exchanged many letters. For more information about Dr. Jenkins, see John M. Hyson, Jr., "Dr. Newell Sill Jenkins: Progenitor of Cosmetic Dentistry," Journal of the California Dental Association,Vol. 31, No. 8, August, 2003, pp. 626-629. Dr. Jenkins was the grandfather of the American conductor Newell Jenkins (1915-1996).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists chiefly of correspondence between the families of the composer Richard Wagner and Dr. Newell Sill Jenkins. The Wagner family is represented by letters from Richard Wagner, his wife Cosima, and their children Siegfried and Eva, as well as Daniela von Bülow (Cosima's daughter by her previous marriage to Hans von Bülow). Only two of the letters are by Richard Wagner, but they are of particular interest; both dated 1880, they discuss his interest in visiting the United States or even moving there permanently, explaining that he would require a million dollars to do so. Jenkins tried to support Wagner's schemes, but they never came to fruition.

The original letters are accompanied by typed transcriptions, as well as English translations of the German-language letters. The collection also includes two Bayreuth performance schedules and two 20th-century articles.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in two series: I. Correspondence. II. Miscellaneous printed material.

did.unittitle: Correspondence

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged chronologically, with undated items filed at the end.

did.unittitle: Inventory

From Cosima Wagner to Mr. Jenkins
From C. Wagner to Mrs. Jenkins
From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins
From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins
From Richard Wagner [to N.S. Jenkins]

From [N.S. Jenkins] to A.D. White
From Richard Wagner and C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins
Draft of a letter from [N.S. Jenkins] to Wagner
From Kingsley Twining to Dr. Jenkins
From J.S. Dwight to Dr. N.S. Jenkins

From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins

From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins

From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins

From C. Wagner to Mr. Jenkins

From Daniela v. Bülow to [N.S. Jenkins]
From C. Wagner to Mrs. Jenkins

Miscellaneous printed material
Bühnenfestspiele in Bayreuth.
[Bühnenfestspiele in Bayreuth]

odd.head: General
odd.p: Calendar of rehearsals and performances. The opera house and date are not named in this fragment, but it appears to be for the Bayreuth Festival in 1889.

did.unittitle: Klaus Liepman, "Wagner's Proposal to America," High Fidelity, December 1975, pp. 70-72.
Bernard Levin, "The Unspeakable Wagners,"Times [of London]
